CONTEXT: 50 convoys currently piled up. Refinery processing one MR at a time. Both gastown and beads refineries are spending minutes on test suites per MR.","design":"# Refinery Parallel Merge Pipeline — Design Document

## Status: PROPOSED
## Author: polecat/slit
## Issue: gt-yxx0

---

## 1. Problem Statement

The Refinery is a single-threaded bottleneck. It processes one MR at a time:
claim, rebase, setup, typecheck, lint, build, test, merge, push — sequentially.

At swarm scale (8+ polecats), MRs queue up and branches go stale waiting.
Current throughput: ~4 MRs/hour (at 15min test suites). Target: 15-20+ MRs/hour.

Current bottleneck breakdown (per MR):
- Rebase: ~5s
- Setup (pnpm install, etc.): ~30s
- Typecheck: ~30s
- Lint: ~20s
- Build: ~60s
- Tests: 5-15 min (DOMINANT COST)
- Merge + push: ~5s

## 2. Proposed Architecture: Batched Merge Queue

### 2.1 Core Design: Batch-then-Bisect (Bors Pattern)

Replace sequential per-MR testing with batched verification:

CURRENT (sequential):
  MR1: rebase then test then merge then push
  MR2: rebase then test then merge then push  (waits for MR1)
  MR3: rebase then test then merge then push  (waits for MR2)
  Total: 3 x test_time

PROPOSED (batched):
  Batch [MR1, MR2, MR3]:
    1. Rebase MR1 onto main
    2. Rebase MR2 onto MR1
    3. Rebase MR3 onto MR2 (this is the "stack tip")
    4. Run tests ONCE on the stack tip
    5. If pass: fast-forward merge all 3
    6. If fail: bisect to find culprit
  Total: 1 x test_time (happy path)

### 2.2 Batch Formation

Config knobs (new fields on MergeQueueConfig):

    MaxBatchSize int           // Default: 5. Larger = more throughput,
                               // bisection cost grows O(log N).
    BatchWaitTime time.Duration // Default: 30s. Wait for batch to fill.
                               // 0 = process immediately.
    MaxParallelBatches int     // Default: 1 (no speculation).
                               // 2-3 for high throughput.

Batch assembly algorithm:
1. Poll queue for open MRs, sorted by score (existing scoring logic)
2. Take up to MaxBatchSize MRs from the head of the queue
3. If queue has fewer MRs than MaxBatchSize and BatchWaitTime > 0,
   wait for more MRs to arrive (up to BatchWaitTime)
4. Claim all MRs in the batch atomically
5. Build the rebase stack (see 2.3)

### 2.3 Rebase Stack Construction

The stack is built bottom-up. Each MR in the batch is rebased onto the
result of all preceding MRs:

    main <- MR1 <- MR2 <- MR3 (stack tip)

Implementation:
    buildStack(ctx, batch []MR, targetBranch) -> (tipSHA, error):
        stackBranch = "refinery/batch/<batchID>"
        git checkout -b stackBranch origin/targetBranch

        for each mr in batch:
            err = git rebase mr.Branch onto stackBranch
            if err (conflict):
                return ConflictError{MR: mr}
            // Stack branch now includes this MR's changes

        return git rev-parse HEAD

Conflict during stack build:
- If MR[i] conflicts during rebase, remove it from the batch
- Assign conflict back to worker (existing flow)
- Rebuild stack without MR[i] (remaining MRs may now be conflict-free)
- File conflict-resolution task as today

### 2.4 Test-on-Tip Verification

Run the full gate suite (typecheck, lint, build, test) on the stack tip.
The tip SHA incorporates ALL MR changes, so a green result proves the batch
is safe to merge.

    verifyBatch(ctx, stackBranch, config) -> BatchResult:
        result = runGates(ctx)  // Existing gate infrastructure
        if result.Success:
            return BatchPassed
        else:
            return BatchFailed{GateResults: result.Details}

### 2.5 Fast-Forward Merge (Happy Path)

When tests pass on the stack tip:
1. Acquire merge slot (existing mechanism)
2. Verify target branch hasn't moved since stack was built
   - If moved: rebuild stack from new target, re-test (retry)
   - If same: proceed to merge
3. git merge --ff-only stackBranch  (fast-forward target to stack tip)
4. git push origin targetBranch
5. Close all MR beads in batch (reason: merged)
6. Close all source issues
7. Delete merged branches
8. Release merge slot

Why fast-forward: The stack tip SHA is exactly what was tested. No merge
commit is created, so what lands is bit-for-bit what CI verified. This is
the "Not Rocket Science Rule" from bors.

### 2.6 Bisection (Failure Path)

When tests fail on the stack tip, bisect to find the culprit:

    bisectBatch(ctx, batch []MR, target) -> culprits []MR:
        if len(batch) == 1:
            return batch  // Single MR failed — it's the culprit

        mid = len(batch) / 2
        left = batch[:mid]
        right = batch[mid:]

        leftStack = buildStack(ctx, left, target)
        leftResult = verifyBatch(ctx, leftStack)

        if leftResult.Failed:
            return bisectBatch(ctx, left, target)

        // Left half is green — culprit is in right half
        // Test right in context of left (cumulative merge)
        // O(log N) test runs to isolate culprit

Bisection strategy:
- Binary search: O(log N) test runs to find the bad MR
- For batch of 5: worst case 3 test runs (vs 5 sequential)
- Culprit MR gets assigned back to worker (existing flow)
- Remaining good MRs get re-batched in next cycle

Optimization — parallel bisection:
When bisecting, test both halves concurrently. The failing half gets
further bisected while the passing half gets merged immediately.
Trade-off: 2x CI capacity usage during bisection for 2x faster isolation.

## 3. Polecat-Owned Pre-Verification

### 3.1 Shift-Left: Polecats Run Gates Before Submission

Currently, polecats run tests on their feature branch (step 6 of
mol-polecat-work). The Refinery then re-runs the same tests after rebase.
This is redundant when no conflicts exist.

Proposed change to mol-polecat-work formula — add step 6.5:

    Step 6.5: Pre-merge rebase verification
      1. Fetch latest target branch
      2. Rebase feature branch onto target
      3. Run full gate suite on rebased result
      4. If pass: submit MR with "pre-verified" flag
      5. If fail (conflict): resolve and retry
      6. If fail (tests): fix and retry

MR metadata (new struct embedded in MR bead):

    type MRPreVerification struct {
        Verified     bool        // polecat ran gates after rebase
        VerifiedAt   time.Time   // when verification completed
        VerifiedBase string      // target branch SHA at verification time
        GateResults  []GateResult // individual gate outcomes
    }

### 3.2 Refinery Fast Path for Pre-Verified MRs

When the Refinery encounters a pre-verified MR whose VerifiedBase
matches the current target HEAD:
- Skip all gates (typecheck, lint, build, test)
- Just fast-forward merge and push
- This reduces Refinery processing to ~5 seconds per MR

When VerifiedBase doesn't match (main moved):
- The polecat's verification is stale
- Refinery must re-verify OR assign back for re-verification
- Decision: if the only changes to main since VerifiedBase are from
  OTHER batched MRs that passed, the pre-verification is still valid
  (transitive trust). Only re-verify if an external push landed on main.

### 3.3 Optimistic Concurrency: "Polecats Fight Over Rebasing"

For maximum throughput, multiple polecats can speculatively rebase and
verify concurrently:

    Polecat A: fetch main -> rebase -> test -> submit (verified_base=abc)
    Polecat B: fetch main -> rebase -> test -> submit (verified_base=abc)
    Polecat C: fetch main -> rebase -> test -> submit (verified_base=abc)

    Refinery: A arrives first, verified_base=abc matches -> merge -> main=def
              B arrives, verified_base=abc != def -> STALE
              C arrives, verified_base=abc != def -> STALE

B and C wasted their test runs. This is the OCC waste problem.

Mitigation: The Refinery batching subsumes this. Polecats pre-verify
against the target they know. The Refinery then batches all pre-verified
MRs, does a quick stack-build (no conflict = no re-test needed thanks to
pre-verification), and fast-forward merges the batch. Only if conflicts
arise during stack-build does re-testing become necessary.

## 4. Review Plugins as Parallel Tasks

### 4.1 Current State

The Refinery runs review plugins inline during MR processing. This adds
latency to the critical path.

### 4.2 Proposed: Decouple Review from Merge

    MR submitted -> Dispatcher
                        |
           +------------+------------+
           v            v            v
      Review       Security      Style
      Polecat      Scanner       Checker
           |            |            |
           v            v            v
      Verdicts (written to beads)
                        |
                        v
                   Refinery (reads verdicts, merges if all pass)

Implementation:
- On MR submission, dispatch review tasks as separate beads
- Each review task is handled by a short-lived polecat (or dog)
- Review polecats write verdicts to the MR bead (pass/fail + notes)
- Refinery waits for all verdicts before proceeding to merge
- Reviews run concurrently with each other AND with test verification

Benefits:
- Reviews don't block the merge queue
- Multiple reviews run in parallel
- Expensive reviews (AI code review) don't delay simple merges
- Reviews are recorded as beads (audit trail)

Trade-off: More agent spawns = more resource usage. For simple
projects, inline review may be faster than spawn overhead.

Config (new struct):

    type ReviewConfig struct {
        Plugins        []ReviewPlugin // review plugins to run
        RequireAllPass bool           // all must pass before merge (default true)
        Timeout        time.Duration  // max wait for all reviews
    }

## 5. Merge Queue Ordering with Parallel Verification

### 5.1 Ordering Strategy: Priority-Scored FIFO with Batching

The existing ScoreMR function already provides good ordering. With batching,
the batch is assembled from the highest-scored MRs in the queue.

Within a batch: Order matters for the rebase stack. Higher-priority MRs
go first (bottom of stack) so they merge even if later MRs in the batch
fail during bisection.

Cross-batch (speculative execution, Phase 4): If MaxParallelBatches > 1:
- Batch 1: MRs [1,2,3] — verified against current main
- Batch 2: MRs [4,5,6] — verified against (main + batch 1 assumed green)
- If batch 1 passes: batch 2's result is immediately valid
- If batch 1 fails: batch 2 is invalidated and must re-verify

This is the Mergify speculative execution model.

### 5.2 Dependency-Aware Batching

MRs within the same epic/convoy should batch together when possible.
MRs with cross-issue dependencies must respect ordering constraints.

    assembleBatch(queue []MR, config BatchConfig) -> Batch:
        batch = []MR{}
        for each mr in queue:
            if len(batch) >= config.MaxBatchSize:
                break
            if mr.blockedBy any MR not in batch:
                continue  // Can't include yet
            batch = append(batch, mr)
        return batch

## 6. Interaction with Witness Decoupling

With polecat-owned pre-verification and batched merging, the Refinery's
remaining responsibilities shrink to:

1. Queue management: Assemble batches from scored queue
2. Stack construction: Rebase MRs in order, detect conflicts
3. Verification (when needed): Run gates on stack tip for non-pre-verified MRs
4. Atomic merge+push: Fast-forward merge and push (serialized by merge slot)
5. Bisection: Isolate culprits when batch verification fails
6. Bookkeeping: Close MR beads, close issues, delete branches

The Refinery becomes a lightweight coordinator. The compute-heavy work
(testing) shifts to polecats (pre-verification) or is amortized across
batches (test-on-tip).

What the Witness gains:
- No longer needs to nudge Refinery about stuck MRs (batches clear faster)
- Zombie detection is simpler (Refinery processes in predictable cycles)
- Polecat completion is self-contained (pre-verify before submission)

## 7. Migration Path

### Phase 1: Intra-MR Gate Parallelism (LOW RISK)
- Set GatesParallel: true in MergeQueueConfig
- This already exists but defaults to false
- Impact: ~2x speedup on gate suite (test + lint + typecheck run concurrently)
- No architectural changes needed

### Phase 2: Batch-then-Bisect (MEDIUM RISK)
- Implement batch assembly and rebase stack construction
- Implement test-on-tip verification
- Implement binary bisection for failures
- Config: BatchConfig{MaxBatchSize: 3, BatchWaitTime: 15s}
- Start conservative (batch of 3), increase as confidence grows
- Fallback: if batch fails, fall back to sequential processing

### Phase 3: Polecat Pre-Verification (MEDIUM RISK)
- Add pre-merge rebase step to mol-polecat-work formula
- Add MRPreVerification metadata to MR beads
- Implement fast-path in Refinery for pre-verified MRs
- Polecats do more work; Refinery does less

### Phase 4: Speculative Parallel Batches (HIGH COMPLEXITY)
- Implement MaxParallelBatches > 1
- Implement speculative stack construction
- Implement invalidation when predecessor batch fails
- Requires careful orchestration of concurrent git operations
- Consider: each parallel batch needs its own worktree

### Phase 5: Review Plugin Parallelism (OPTIONAL)
- Decouple review plugins from merge pipeline
- Dispatch reviews as parallel polecat/dog tasks
- Implement verdict collection and gate-checking
- Only needed if review latency becomes a bottleneck

## 8. Trade-offs

| Aspect | Current | Proposed (Phase 2+3) | Risk |
|--------|---------|---------------------|------|
| Throughput | ~4 MR/hr | ~15-20 MR/hr | - |
| Complexity | Simple sequential loop | Batch assembly + bisection | Medium |
| Test coverage | Each MR tested independently | Test-on-tip (batch) | Merge-skew caught by cumulative rebase |
| Failure isolation | Immediate (1 MR = 1 test) | O(log N) bisection | Slightly slower for failures |
| Resource usage | 1 test run per MR | 1 test run per batch (happy path) | Lower total CI cost |
| Polecat cost | Tests on feature branch only | Tests on feature + rebased | Higher per-polecat cost |
| Git operations | Simple rebase per MR | Rebase stack construction | More complex but well-understood |
| Merge slot | Held during entire MR processing | Held only during push | Shorter lock duration |

## 9. Key Design Decisions

1. **Batch-then-bisect over speculative parallel:** Start with batching (Phase 2)
   before adding speculation (Phase 4). Batching gives 3-5x throughput with
   moderate complexity. Speculation adds another 2-3x but with high complexity.

2. **Pre-verification as complement, not replacement:** Polecat pre-verification
   reduces Refinery work but doesn't eliminate it. The Refinery is still the
   single writer to main (merge slot). Pre-verification is an optimization, not
   a requirement.

3. **Fast-forward merge semantics:** What you test is what you land. No merge
   commits that could introduce untested state. This is the bors guarantee.

4. **Existing infrastructure reuse:** The GatesParallel flag, merge slot,
   MR scoring, and conflict-resolution task patterns all carry forward.
   The batch layer wraps around the existing gate infrastructure.

5. **Pluggable test command:** The test command is already configurable via
   MergeQueueConfig.TestCommand. Batched verification uses the same command,
   just runs it on the stack tip instead of individual branches.

## 10. Open Questions

1. **Stack depth limit:** How deep can the rebase stack go before conflicts
   become likely? Empirical data needed. Start with MaxBatchSize=3-5.

2. **Bisection vs sequential fallback:** When a batch fails, is bisection
   always better than falling back to sequential testing? For batch=2,
   bisection IS sequential. Bisection wins at batch>=4.

3. **Cross-rig batching:** Should the gastown and beads refineries share
   a batch? No — they operate on different repos. But they could share
   the merge slot (already the case).

4. **Integration branch batching:** When MRs target an integration branch
   instead of main, batching still applies. The stack is built against
   the integration branch HEAD. Landing the integration branch to main
   is a separate (non-batched) operation.

5. **Flaky test handling in batches:** If a batch fails due to a flaky test,
   bisection would incorrectly blame an innocent MR. Mitigation: retry the
   full batch once before bisecting (existing RetryFlakyTests config applies
   at the batch level).","notes":"ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENT: Design should include a pluggable merge queue with batched test runs as the default. Batch N branches, rebase as a stack, run tests once on the tip. If tests pass, fast-forward merge the batch. If they fail, bisect to find the culprit. Ship a default (go test ./... on batch tip) but make it pluggable for custom CI. This is the standard merge queue pattern (Bors, GitHub merge queues, Mergify) adapted for Gas Town.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"gastown/polecats/slit","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T03:15:24Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T03:23:41Z"}

AssessHelpRequest (protocol.go:410-457) is a full Go-level triage engine that pattern-matches on free-text topic/problem fields using strings.Contains ('git', 'conflict', 'test fail', 'build', 'compile', 'unclear'). It makes CanHelp/NeedsEscalation decisions and hardcodes 'Mayor' as escalation target. HandleHelp (line 301-321) acts on these decisions directly - the agent never gets to override.

This is the most severe ZFC violation found. It hits rules 2 (hardcoded role names), 3 (judgment calls), and 5 (string pattern matching) simultaneously.

Fix: Remove AssessHelpRequest entirely. HandleHelp should parse the HELP payload (transport) and present it to the witness agent for triage. {"description":"PROBLEM: The refinery is a single-threaded bottleneck. It processes one MR at a time: build → test → merge → push, sequentially. At swarm scale (8+ polecats), MRs queue up and branches go stale waiting. The refinery runs full test suites itself, blocking the merge queue for minutes per MR.

DESIRED STATE: The refinery becomes a lightweight coordinator. Expensive work (build, test, lint, review) is parallelized across helpers or done by the polecats themselves before submission.

DESIGN INVESTIGATION:
1. **Refinery helpers (Dog-like pattern)**: The Deacon offloads work to Dogs. Can the refinery spawn short-lived helpers to run build/test/review in parallel? Multiple MRs verified concurrently, refinery just does the final fast-forward merge.

2. **Polecat-owned rebasing**: Polecats rebase onto main, run tests, and only submit when green. The refinery's job shrinks to: verify the branch is rebased, fast-forward merge, push. If main moved, polecat retries (optimistic concurrency — like real CI). This is the 'polecats fight over rebasing' model.

3. **Review plugins as polecats**: Instead of the refinery running review plugins inline, dispatch them as polecat tasks. Code review, security scan, style check — all run concurrently by different polecats, results written to beads. Refinery reads the verdict.

4. **Merge queue ordering**: With parallel verification, how does the refinery decide merge order? First-green-wins? Priority-based? Dependency-aware?

5. **Interaction with witness decoupling (gt-0wkk)**: If polecats self-manage completion AND self-manage rebasing, what's left for the refinery? Just the atomic merge+push?

DELIVERABLE: Design doc with proposed architecture, migration path, and tradeoffs. Not code.

CONTEXT: 50 convoys currently piled up. Refinery processing one MR at a time. Both gastown and beads refineries are spending minutes on test suites per MR."}

{"id":"gt-yxx0","title":"Design: Refinery parallel merge pipeline — helpers and polecat-owned rebasing","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-4bijs

If main moved, polecat retries (optimistic concurrency — like real CI). This is the 'polecats fight over rebasing' model.

CONTEXT: 50 convoys currently piled up. Refinery processing one MR at a time. Both gastown and beads refineries are spending minutes on test suites per MR.","design":"# Refinery Parallel Merge Pipeline — Design Document

## Status: PROPOSED
## Author: polecat/slit
## Issue: gt-yxx0

---

## 1. Problem Statement

The Refinery is a single-threaded bottleneck. It processes one MR at a time:
claim, rebase, setup, typecheck, lint, build, test, merge, push — sequentially.

At swarm scale (8+ polecats), MRs queue up and branches go stale waiting.
Current throughput: ~4 MRs/hour (at 15min test suites). Target: 15-20+ MRs/hour.

Current bottleneck breakdown (per MR):
- Rebase: ~5s
- Setup (pnpm install, etc.): ~30s
- Typecheck: ~30s
- Lint: ~20s
- Build: ~60s
- Tests: 5-15 min (DOMINANT COST)
- Merge + push: ~5s

## 2. Proposed Architecture: Batched Merge Queue

### 2.1 Core Design: Batch-then-Bisect (Bors Pattern)

Replace sequential per-MR testing with batched verification:

CURRENT (sequential):
  MR1: rebase then test then merge then push
  MR2: rebase then test then merge then push  (waits for MR1)
  MR3: rebase then test then merge then push  (waits for MR2)
  Total: 3 x test_time

PROPOSED (batched):
  Batch [MR1, MR2, MR3]:
    1. Rebase MR1 onto main
    2. Rebase MR2 onto MR1
    3. Rebase MR3 onto MR2 (this is the "stack tip")
    4. Run tests ONCE on the stack tip
    5. If pass: fast-forward merge all 3
    6. If fail: bisect to find culprit
  Total: 1 x test_time (happy path)

### 2.2 Batch Formation

Config knobs (new fields on MergeQueueConfig):

    MaxBatchSize int           // Default: 5. Larger = more throughput,
                               // bisection cost grows O(log N).
    BatchWaitTime time.Duration // Default: 30s. Wait for batch to fill.
                               // 0 = process immediately.
    MaxParallelBatches int     // Default: 1 (no speculation).
                               // 2-3 for high throughput.

Batch assembly algorithm:
1. Poll queue for open MRs, sorted by score (existing scoring logic)
2. Take up to MaxBatchSize MRs from the head of the queue
3. If queue has fewer MRs than MaxBatchSize and BatchWaitTime > 0,
   wait for more MRs to arrive (up to BatchWaitTime)
4. Claim all MRs in the batch atomically
5. Build the rebase stack (see 2.3)

### 2.3 Rebase Stack Construction

The stack is built bottom-up. Each MR in the batch is rebased onto the
result of all preceding MRs:

    main <- MR1 <- MR2 <- MR3 (stack tip)

Implementation:
    buildStack(ctx, batch []MR, targetBranch) -> (tipSHA, error):
        stackBranch = "refinery/batch/<batchID>"
        git checkout -b stackBranch origin/targetBranch

        for each mr in batch:
            err = git rebase mr.Branch onto stackBranch
            if err (conflict):
                return ConflictError{MR: mr}
            // Stack branch now includes this MR's changes

        return git rev-parse HEAD

Conflict during stack build:
- If MR[i] conflicts during rebase, remove it from the batch
- Assign conflict back to worker (existing flow)
- Rebuild stack without MR[i] (remaining MRs may now be conflict-free)
- File conflict-resolution task as today

### 2.4 Test-on-Tip Verification

Run the full gate suite (typecheck, lint, build, test) on the stack tip.
The tip SHA incorporates ALL MR changes, so a green result proves the batch
is safe to merge.

    verifyBatch(ctx, stackBranch, config) -> BatchResult:
        result = runGates(ctx)  // Existing gate infrastructure
        if result.Success:
            return BatchPassed
        else:
            return BatchFailed{GateResults: result.Details}

### 2.5 Fast-Forward Merge (Happy Path)

When tests pass on the stack tip:
1. Acquire merge slot (existing mechanism)
2. Verify target branch hasn't moved since stack was built
   - If moved: rebuild stack from new target, re-test (retry)
   - If same: proceed to merge
3. git merge --ff-only stackBranch  (fast-forward target to stack tip)
4. git push origin targetBranch
5. Close all MR beads in batch (reason: merged)
6. Close all source issues
7. Delete merged branches
8. Release merge slot

Why fast-forward: The stack tip SHA is exactly what was tested. No merge
commit is created, so what lands is bit-for-bit what CI verified. This is
the "Not Rocket Science Rule" from bors.

### 2.6 Bisection (Failure Path)

When tests fail on the stack tip, bisect to find the culprit:

    bisectBatch(ctx, batch []MR, target) -> culprits []MR:
        if len(batch) == 1:
            return batch  // Single MR failed — it's the culprit

        mid = len(batch) / 2
        left = batch[:mid]
        right = batch[mid:]

        leftStack = buildStack(ctx, left, target)
        leftResult = verifyBatch(ctx, leftStack)

        if leftResult.Failed:
            return bisectBatch(ctx, left, target)

        // Left half is green — culprit is in right half
        // Test right in context of left (cumulative merge)
        // O(log N) test runs to isolate culprit

Bisection strategy:
- Binary search: O(log N) test runs to find the bad MR
- For batch of 5: worst case 3 test runs (vs 5 sequential)
- Culprit MR gets assigned back to worker (existing flow)
- Remaining good MRs get re-batched in next cycle

Optimization — parallel bisection:
When bisecting, test both halves concurrently. The failing half gets
further bisected while the passing half gets merged immediately.
Trade-off: 2x CI capacity usage during bisection for 2x faster isolation.

## 3. Polecat-Owned Pre-Verification

### 3.1 Shift-Left: Polecats Run Gates Before Submission

Currently, polecats run tests on their feature branch (step 6 of
mol-polecat-work). The Refinery then re-runs the same tests after rebase.
This is redundant when no conflicts exist.

Proposed change to mol-polecat-work formula — add step 6.5:

    Step 6.5: Pre-merge rebase verification
      1. Fetch latest target branch
      2. Rebase feature branch onto target
      3. Run full gate suite on rebased result
      4. If pass: submit MR with "pre-verified" flag
      5. If fail (conflict): resolve and retry
      6. If fail (tests): fix and retry

MR metadata (new struct embedded in MR bead):

    type MRPreVerification struct {
        Verified     bool        // polecat ran gates after rebase
        VerifiedAt   time.Time   // when verification completed
        VerifiedBase string      // target branch SHA at verification time
        GateResults  []GateResult // individual gate outcomes
    }

### 3.2 Refinery Fast Path for Pre-Verified MRs

When the Refinery encounters a pre-verified MR whose VerifiedBase
matches the current target HEAD:
- Skip all gates (typecheck, lint, build, test)
- Just fast-forward merge and push
- This reduces Refinery processing to ~5 seconds per MR

When VerifiedBase doesn't match (main moved):
- The polecat's verification is stale
- Refinery must re-verify OR assign back for re-verification
- Decision: if the only changes to main since VerifiedBase are from
  OTHER batched MRs that passed, the pre-verification is still valid
  (transitive trust). Only re-verify if an external push landed on main.

### 3.3 Optimistic Concurrency: "Polecats Fight Over Rebasing"

For maximum throughput, multiple polecats can speculatively rebase and
verify concurrently:

    Polecat A: fetch main -> rebase -> test -> submit (verified_base=abc)
    Polecat B: fetch main -> rebase -> test -> submit (verified_base=abc)
    Polecat C: fetch main -> rebase -> test -> submit (verified_base=abc)

    Refinery: A arrives first, verified_base=abc matches -> merge -> main=def
              B arrives, verified_base=abc != def -> STALE
              C arrives, verified_base=abc != def -> STALE

B and C wasted their test runs. This is the OCC waste problem.

Mitigation: The Refinery batching subsumes this. Polecats pre-verify
against the target they know. The Refinery then batches all pre-verified
MRs, does a quick stack-build (no conflict = no re-test needed thanks to
pre-verification), and fast-forward merges the batch. Only if conflicts
arise during stack-build does re-testing become necessary.

## 4. Review Plugins as Parallel Tasks

### 4.1 Current State

The Refinery runs review plugins inline during MR processing. This adds
latency to the critical path.

### 4.2 Proposed: Decouple Review from Merge

    MR submitted -> Dispatcher
                        |
           +------------+------------+
           v            v            v
      Review       Security      Style
      Polecat      Scanner       Checker
           |            |            |
           v            v            v
      Verdicts (written to beads)
                        |
                        v
                   Refinery (reads verdicts, merges if all pass)

Implementation:
- On MR submission, dispatch review tasks as separate beads
- Each review task is handled by a short-lived polecat (or dog)
- Review polecats write verdicts to the MR bead (pass/fail + notes)
- Refinery waits for all verdicts before proceeding to merge
- Reviews run concurrently with each other AND with test verification

Benefits:
- Reviews don't block the merge queue
- Multiple reviews run in parallel
- Expensive reviews (AI code review) don't delay simple merges
- Reviews are recorded as beads (audit trail)

Trade-off: More agent spawns = more resource usage. For simple
projects, inline review may be faster than spawn overhead.

Config (new struct):

    type ReviewConfig struct {
        Plugins        []ReviewPlugin // review plugins to run
        RequireAllPass bool           // all must pass before merge (default true)
        Timeout        time.Duration  // max wait for all reviews
    }

## 5. Merge Queue Ordering with Parallel Verification

### 5.1 Ordering Strategy: Priority-Scored FIFO with Batching

The existing ScoreMR function already provides good ordering. With batching,
the batch is assembled from the highest-scored MRs in the queue.

Within a batch: Order matters for the rebase stack. Higher-priority MRs
go first (bottom of stack) so they merge even if later MRs in the batch
fail during bisection.

Cross-batch (speculative execution, Phase 4): If MaxParallelBatches > 1:
- Batch 1: MRs [1,2,3] — verified against current main
- Batch 2: MRs [4,5,6] — verified against (main + batch 1 assumed green)
- If batch 1 passes: batch 2's result is immediately valid
- If batch 1 fails: batch 2 is invalidated and must re-verify

This is the Mergify speculative execution model.

### 5.2 Dependency-Aware Batching

MRs within the same epic/convoy should batch together when possible.
MRs with cross-issue dependencies must respect ordering constraints.

    assembleBatch(queue []MR, config BatchConfig) -> Batch:
        batch = []MR{}
        for each mr in queue:
            if len(batch) >= config.MaxBatchSize:
                break
            if mr.blockedBy any MR not in batch:
                continue  // Can't include yet
            batch = append(batch, mr)
        return batch

## 6. Interaction with Witness Decoupling

With polecat-owned pre-verification and batched merging, the Refinery's
remaining responsibilities shrink to:

1. Queue management: Assemble batches from scored queue
2. Stack construction: Rebase MRs in order, detect conflicts
3. Verification (when needed): Run gates on stack tip for non-pre-verified MRs
4. Atomic merge+push: Fast-forward merge and push (serialized by merge slot)
5. Bisection: Isolate culprits when batch verification fails
6. Bookkeeping: Close MR beads, close issues, delete branches

The Refinery becomes a lightweight coordinator. The compute-heavy work
(testing) shifts to polecats (pre-verification) or is amortized across
batches (test-on-tip).

What the Witness gains:
- No longer needs to nudge Refinery about stuck MRs (batches clear faster)
- Zombie detection is simpler (Refinery processes in predictable cycles)
- Polecat completion is self-contained (pre-verify before submission)

## 7. Migration Path

### Phase 1: Intra-MR Gate Parallelism (LOW RISK)
- Set GatesParallel: true in MergeQueueConfig
- This already exists but defaults to false
- Impact: ~2x speedup on gate suite (test + lint + typecheck run concurrently)
- No architectural changes needed

### Phase 2: Batch-then-Bisect (MEDIUM RISK)
- Implement batch assembly and rebase stack construction
- Implement test-on-tip verification
- Implement binary bisection for failures
- Config: BatchConfig{MaxBatchSize: 3, BatchWaitTime: 15s}
- Start conservative (batch of 3), increase as confidence grows
- Fallback: if batch fails, fall back to sequential processing

### Phase 3: Polecat Pre-Verification (MEDIUM RISK)
- Add pre-merge rebase step to mol-polecat-work formula
- Add MRPreVerification metadata to MR beads
- Implement fast-path in Refinery for pre-verified MRs
- Polecats do more work; Refinery does less

### Phase 4: Speculative Parallel Batches (HIGH COMPLEXITY)
- Implement MaxParallelBatches > 1
- Implement speculative stack construction
- Implement invalidation when predecessor batch fails
- Requires careful orchestration of concurrent git operations
- Consider: each parallel batch needs its own worktree

### Phase 5: Review Plugin Parallelism (OPTIONAL)
- Decouple review plugins from merge pipeline
- Dispatch reviews as parallel polecat/dog tasks
- Implement verdict collection and gate-checking
- Only needed if review latency becomes a bottleneck

## 8. Trade-offs

| Aspect | Current | Proposed (Phase 2+3) | Risk |
|--------|---------|---------------------|------|
| Throughput | ~4 MR/hr | ~15-20 MR/hr | - |
| Complexity | Simple sequential loop | Batch assembly + bisection | Medium |
| Test coverage | Each MR tested independently | Test-on-tip (batch) | Merge-skew caught by cumulative rebase |
| Failure isolation | Immediate (1 MR = 1 test) | O(log N) bisection | Slightly slower for failures |
| Resource usage | 1 test run per MR | 1 test run per batch (happy path) | Lower total CI cost |
| Polecat cost | Tests on feature branch only | Tests on feature + rebased | Higher per-polecat cost |
| Git operations | Simple rebase per MR | Rebase stack construction | More complex but well-understood |
| Merge slot | Held during entire MR processing | Held only during push | Shorter lock duration |

## 9. Key Design Decisions

1. **Batch-then-bisect over speculative parallel:** Start with batching (Phase 2)
   before adding speculation (Phase 4). Batching gives 3-5x throughput with
   moderate complexity. Speculation adds another 2-3x but with high complexity.

2. **Pre-verification as complement, not replacement:** Polecat pre-verification
   reduces Refinery work but doesn't eliminate it. The Refinery is still the
   single writer to main (merge slot). Pre-verification is an optimization, not
   a requirement.

3. **Fast-forward merge semantics:** What you test is what you land. No merge
   commits that could introduce untested state. This is the bors guarantee.

4. **Existing infrastructure reuse:** The GatesParallel flag, merge slot,
   MR scoring, and conflict-resolution task patterns all carry forward.
   The batch layer wraps around the existing gate infrastructure.

5. **Pluggable test command:** The test command is already configurable via
   MergeQueueConfig.TestCommand. Batched verification uses the same command,
   just runs it on the stack tip instead of individual branches.

## 10. Open Questions

1. **Stack depth limit:** How deep can the rebase stack go before conflicts
   become likely? Empirical data needed. Start with MaxBatchSize=3-5.

2. **Bisection vs sequential fallback:** When a batch fails, is bisection
   always better than falling back to sequential testing? For batch=2,
   bisection IS sequential. Bisection wins at batch>=4.

3. **Cross-rig batching:** Should the gastown and beads refineries share
   a batch? No — they operate on different repos. But they could share
   the merge slot (already the case).

4. **Integration branch batching:** When MRs target an integration branch
   instead of main, batching still applies. The stack is built against
   the integration branch HEAD. Landing the integration branch to main
   is a separate (non-batched) operation.

5. **Flaky test handling in batches:** If a batch fails due to a flaky test,
   bisection would incorrectly blame an innocent MR. Mitigation: retry the
   full batch once before bisecting (existing RetryFlakyTests config applies
   at the batch level).","notes":"Design complete. Key recommendations:
1. Phase 1 (immediate): Enable GatesParallel=true for ~2x gate speedup
2. Phase 2 (core): Implement batch-then-bisect (bors pattern) — batch N MRs, rebase as stack, test tip, bisect on failure. 3-5x throughput.
3. Phase 3: Polecat pre-verification — polecats rebase+test before submission, Refinery fast-paths pre-verified MRs.
4. Phase 4: Speculative parallel batches (Mergify model) — concurrent batch verification for maximum throughput.
5. Phase 5 (optional): Decouple review plugins as parallel polecat/dog tasks.
Key files: engineer.go (1697 lines), manager.go, types.go, score.go, done.go.
Existing infrastructure (merge slot, GatesParallel, scoring) carries forward.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"gastown/polecats/slit","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T03:15:24Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T03:24:10Z","closed_at":"2026-02-28T03:24:10Z","close_reason":"Completed with no code changes (already fixed or pushed directly to main)"}

{"id":"gt-idrl","title":"ZFC: witness DetectStalledPolecats screen-scrapes tmux pane content","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-c91dr
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T03:43:32Z
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

DetectStalledPolecats (handlers.go:1485-1514) captures 30 lines of tmux pane content and does strings.Contains matching on UI text: 'trust this folder', 'Quick safety check', 'Bypass Permissions mode'. These are third-party Claude Code TUI strings that could change with any update.

This is the second most severe ZFC violation - literal screen-scraping of another tool's display output.

Fix: Handle at startup level (ensure --dangerously-skip-permissions is always passed). If stall detection needed, use structured signals (session age vs first tool call timestamp), not raw screen scraping.","notes":"Implemented: Replaced screen-scraping (CapturePane + strings.Contains) in DetectStalledPolecats with structured signals (GetSessionCreatedUnix + GetSessionActivity). Sessions >90s old with no recent tmux activity are flagged as startup-stall and escalated via AcceptStartupDialogs. No more matching against third-party TUI strings. Updated tests accordingly.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T02:49:19Z","created_by":"gastown/polecats/nux","updated_at":"2026-02-28T03:46:02Z","closed_at":"2026-02-28T03:46:02Z","close_reason":"Closed"}

new_value: {"notes":"Implemented: Removed AssessHelpRequest (Go-level triage engine), HelpAssessment type, and escalateToDeacon (dead code). HandleHelp now only parses HELP payload and presents it to the witness agent via FormatHelpSummary for agent-level triage. Replaced 5 old tests with 2 new FormatHelpSummary tests. All witness tests pass, full suite green (pre-existing TestCrossPlatformBuild and TestVerifyStartupNudgeDelivery_IdleAgent failures unrelated)."}
old_value:
{"id":"gt-l7uq","title":"ZFC: witness AssessHelpRequest is a Go-level triage engine","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-huatp
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T03:43:23Z
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

AssessHelpRequest (protocol.go:410-457) is a full Go-level triage engine that pattern-matches on free-text topic/problem fields using strings.Contains ('git', 'conflict', 'test fail', 'build', 'compile', 'unclear'). It makes CanHelp/NeedsEscalation decisions and hardcodes 'Mayor' as escalation target. HandleHelp (line 301-321) acts on these decisions directly - the agent never gets to override.

This is the most severe ZFC violation found. It hits rules 2 (hardcoded role names), 3 (judgment calls), and 5 (string pattern matching) simultaneously.

Fix: Remove AssessHelpRequest entirely. HandleHelp should parse the HELP payload (transport) and present it to the witness agent for triage. Agent decides whether to help, escalate, and to whom.","notes":"Implemented: Removed AssessHelpRequest (Go-level triage engine), HelpAssessment type, and escalateToDeacon (dead code). HandleHelp now only parses HELP payload and presents it to the witness agent via FormatHelpSummary for agent-level triage. Replaced 5 old tests with 2 new FormatHelpSummary tests. All witness tests pass, full suite green (pre-existing TestCrossPlatformBuild failure on main unrelated).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"gastown/polecats/furiosa","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T02:49:16Z","created_by":"gastown/polecats/nux","updated_at":"2026-02-28T03:49:26Z","closed_at":"2026-02-28T03:49:26Z","close_reason":"Closed"}

Per dolt-storage.md, the Reaper Dog should DELETE FROM wisps WHERE status='closed' AND age > 7d. Currently the reaper plugin exists but does not delete rows — it only closes stale wisps. The DECAY stage of the six-stage lifecycle is not implemented. Without row deletion, the wisps table grows without bound. After deletion, the Compactor should rebase the commits that wrote those rows."}

{"notes":"Implemented: Speed up internal/web (~7s→~2.5s) and internal/util (~11s→~3.5s) tests.

Changes:
1. internal/web: Use fast-failing 'false' stub instead of real 'gt' binary for TestAPIHandler_Crew, TestAPIHandler_Ready, and 5 validation wiring tests that only need command execution to fail (not succeed).
2. internal/util: Remove duplicate TestFindOrphanedClaudeProcesses_IgnoresTerminalProcesses which was identical to TestFindOrphanedClaudeProcesses (both called FindOrphanedClaudeProcesses and logged). Merged TTY comment into remaining test.

{"id":"gt-l8dc","title":"Compactor Dog: automate daily DOLT_REBASE to squash old commits","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-gm1ae
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T04:00:30Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Per dolt-storage.md, the Compactor Dog should run DOLT_REBASE daily to squash old commits. This is the COMPACT stage of the six-stage lifecycle. Currently not automated — the only rebase/flatten was done manually on 2026-02-27 (9,195 commits → 6). Commit graphs are already climbing again (380/224/410 across HQ/beads/gastown). Without automated compaction, every bd command pays increasing query tax. Previous hq-w8zg was closed but the automation was never shipped.","status":"hooked","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T03:58:38Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T04:00:30Z"}

Completed with no code changes (already fixed or pushed directly to main)

{"notes":"Implemented: Wrapped all reaper mutations (reap, purge wisps, purge mail, auto-close) in explicit DOLT_COMMIT with auto-commit disabled. {"description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-zpqcb
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T04:28:44Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Implement the core batched merge queue per gt-yxx0 design doc Phase 2. This is built into the refinery core, not a pluggable strategy.

Key components:
1. Batch assembly: poll queue, take up to MaxBatchSize MRs, configurable wait time
2. Rebase stack construction: rebase each MR onto the previous, building main←MR1←MR2←MR3
3. Test-on-tip: run gates once on stack tip (existing gate infra, pluggable test command)
4. Fast-forward merge: if green, ff-merge all MRs in batch atomically
5. Binary bisection: if red, O(log N) test runs to isolate culprit, re-batch good MRs

Config knobs (MergeQueueConfig):
- MaxBatchSize int (default 5)
- BatchWaitTime duration (default 30s)
- RetryBatchOnFlaky bool (default true, retry full batch once before bisecting)

The gates themselves (test command, lint, typecheck) remain pluggable via existing config. The batching/bisection algorithm is invariant core logic.

Reference: gt-yxx0 design doc Phase 2, Section 2.1-2.6."}

{"description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-50lse
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T04:28:56Z
dispatched_by: mayor

When a user runs gt init or gt up for the first time, the six-stage Dolt lifecycle (CREATE → LIVE → CLOSE → DECAY → COMPACT → FLATTEN) should be automatically configured with sensible defaults. This means: 1) Reaper Dog plugin scheduled daily (DELETE closed wisps >7d), 2) Compactor Dog plugin scheduled daily (DOLT_REBASE old commits), 3) Doctor Dog plugin scheduled daily (dolt gc after rebase), 4) JSONL backup plugin on schedule, 5) Dolt filesystem backup every 15min. Users shouldn't have to manually configure maintenance — it should just work out of the box. Make schedules and retention periods configurable via gt config."}

{"id":"gt-i2vm","title":"Phase 2: Batch-then-bisect merge queue (core)","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-peuh0
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work

Implement the core batched merge queue per gt-yxx0 design doc Phase 2. This is built into the refinery core, not a pluggable strategy.

Key components:
1. Batch assembly: poll queue, take up to MaxBatchSize MRs, configurable wait time
2. Rebase stack construction: rebase each MR onto the previous, building main←MR1←MR2←MR3
3. Test-on-tip: run gates once on stack tip (existing gate infra, pluggable test command)
4. Fast-forward merge: if green, ff-merge all MRs in batch atomically
5. Binary bisection: if red, O(log N) test runs to isolate culprit, re-batch good MRs

Config knobs (MergeQueueConfig):
- MaxBatchSize int (default 5)
- BatchWaitTime duration (default 30s)
- RetryBatchOnFlaky bool (default true, retry full batch once before bisecting)

The gates themselves (test command, lint, typecheck) remain pluggable via existing config. The batching/bisection algorithm is invariant core logic.

Reference: gt-yxx0 design doc Phase 2, Section 2.1-2.6.","notes":"Analysis complete. Key implementation plan:
1. Add BatchConfig fields (MaxBatchSize, BatchWaitTime, RetryBatchOnFlaky) to MergeQueueConfig in engineer.go
2. Add batch config parsing to LoadConfig
3. Create batch.go with: Batch type, assembleBatch, buildRebaseStack, verifyBatch, fastForwardMerge, bisectBatch
4. Create batch_test.go with comprehensive unit tests
5. Wire batch processing into Engineer (new ProcessBatch method alongside existing ProcessMRInfo)
Key insight: batch.go wraps existing gate infra (runGates) and merge slot. {"id":"gt-q3or","title":"Auto-configure six-stage lifecycle for new Gas Town installations","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-50lse
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T04:28:56Z
dispatched_by: mayor

When a user runs gt init or gt up for the first time, the six-stage Dolt lifecycle (CREATE → LIVE → CLOSE → DECAY → COMPACT → FLATTEN) should be automatically configured with sensible defaults. This means: 1) Reaper Dog plugin scheduled daily (DELETE closed wisps >7d), 2) Compactor Dog plugin scheduled daily (DOLT_REBASE old commits), 3) Doctor Dog plugin scheduled daily (dolt gc after rebase), 4) JSONL backup plugin on schedule, 5) Dolt filesystem backup every 15min. Users shouldn't have to manually configure maintenance — it should just work out of the box. Make schedules and retention periods configurable via gt config.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"gastown/polecats/rictus","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T04:03:46Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T04:35:22Z","closed_at":"2026-02-28T04:35:22Z","close_reason":"Closed"}

{"id":"gt-th5n","title":"gt sling passes --root-only to bd mol wisp but flag doesn't exist","description":"gt sling internally calls 'bd mol wisp --root-only' when applying mol-polecat-work formula, but bd binary doesn't recognize --root-only flag. This causes formula application to fail silently (polecats get raw bead hook instead of full formula). Every sling falls back to raw bead mode. Need to either: 1) Add --root-only flag to bd mol wisp, or 2) Remove --root-only from gt's formula application call.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T05:07:02Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T05:07:02Z"}

{"id":"gt-idrl","title":"ZFC: witness DetectStalledPolecats screen-scrapes tmux pane content","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-c91dr
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T03:43:32Z
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

DetectStalledPolecats (handlers.go:1485-1514) captures 30 lines of tmux pane content and does strings.Contains matching on UI text: 'trust this folder', 'Quick safety check', 'Bypass Permissions mode'. These are third-party Claude Code TUI strings that could change with any update.

This is the second most severe ZFC violation - literal screen-scraping of another tool's display output.

Fix: Handle at startup level (ensure --dangerously-skip-permissions is always passed). If stall detection needed, use structured signals (session age vs first tool call timestamp), not raw screen scraping.","notes":"Implemented: Replaced screen-scraping (CapturePane + strings.Contains) in DetectStalledPolecats with structured signals (GetSessionCreatedUnix + GetSessionActivity). Sessions >90s old with >60s of tmux inactivity are flagged as startup-stall and remediated via AcceptStartupDialogs. No more matching against third-party TUI strings in the detection logic. Updated tests accordingly.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T02:49:19Z","created_by":"gastown/polecats/nux","updated_at":"2026-02-28T03:51:29Z"}

NOTE: Many switches have role-specific logic that cannot simply be table-driven - need case-by-case analysis. This is a multi-session task."}

{"id":"gt-2e5q","title":"Role taxonomy hardcoded in 15+ switch blocks across 10+ files","description":"attached_formula: mol-polecat-work\ndispatched_by: mayor","design":"REFACTORING PLAN: (1) Consolidate 3 parallel Role constant defs into single authoritative source in internal/config/roles - extend AllRoles/TownRoles/RigRoles with role properties (autonomous, level, session-pattern, env-vars). (2) Create role registry that loads from TOML configs already in internal/config/roles/*.toml. (3) Replace switch blocks with registry lookups - e.g. registry.IsAutonomous(role), registry.SessionName(role), registry.EnvVars(role). (4) Deduplicate claude/settings.go and gemini/settings.go RoleTypeFor(). (5) Start with lowest-risk files (settings.go duplicates, runtime.go) then work outward. TOP-PRIORITY FILES: internal/claude/settings.go, internal/gemini/settings.go (identical duplicates), internal/runtime/runtime.go, internal/config/env.go (largest single switch), internal/daemon/lifecycle.go (6 switches). NOTE: Many switches have role-specific logic that cannot simply be table-driven - need case-by-case analysis. This is a multi-session task.","notes":"AUDIT COMPLETE: Found 35+ files with 60+ switch/case blocks hardcoding role taxonomy. Three parallel role constant definitions exist: (1) internal/session/identity.go - typed Role constants, 7 roles; (2) internal/constants/constants.go - untyped string constants, 6 roles; (3) internal/cmd/role.go - another Role type with Boot/Dog/Unknown added. New TOML-based config in internal/config/roles/*.toml exists but switch blocks remain. Key files by severity: daemon/lifecycle.go (6 switches), cmd/role.go (4+1 if/else), cmd/prime_output.go (4), cmd/nudge.go (4+2), session/identity.go (4+3), cmd/handoff.go (3), cmd/prime.go (3). Also claude/settings.go and gemini/settings.go are exact duplicates. config/roles.go AllRoles() is closest to single source of truth but only used by config loader. {"id":"gt-idrl","title":"ZFC: witness DetectStalledPolecats screen-scrapes tmux pane content","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-c91dr
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T03:43:32Z
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

DetectStalledPolecats (handlers.go:1485-1514) captures 30 lines of tmux pane content and does strings.Contains matching on UI text: 'trust this folder', 'Quick safety check', 'Bypass Permissions mode'. These are third-party Claude Code TUI strings that could change with any update.

This is the second most severe ZFC violation - literal screen-scraping of another tool's display output.

Fix: Handle at startup level (ensure --dangerously-skip-permissions is always passed). If stall detection needed, use structured signals (session age vs first tool call timestamp), not raw screen scraping.","notes":"Implemented: Replaced screen-scraping (CapturePane + strings.Contains) in DetectStalledPolecats with structured signals (GetSessionCreatedUnix + GetSessionActivity). Sessions >90s old with >60s of tmux inactivity are flagged as startup-stall and remediated via AcceptStartupDialogs. No more matching against third-party TUI strings in the detection logic. Updated tests accordingly.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T02:49:19Z","created_by":"gastown/polecats/nux","updated_at":"2026-02-28T05:33:31Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"SESSION PROGRESS (furiosa): Phase 1 complete. (1) Added 'autonomous' and 'emoji' properties to all 7 TOML role configs. (2) Created IsAutonomous() and RoleEmoji() in config package with lazy-loaded registry from embedded TOMLs. Boot handled as deacon variant. (3) Replaced 3 hardcoded switch blocks with config.IsAutonomous(): claude/settings.go RoleTypeFor(), gemini/settings.go RoleTypeFor(), runtime/runtime.go isAutonomousRole(). (4) Added comprehensive tests for IsAutonomous and TOML Autonomous field loading. All tests pass. REMAINING: constants.RoleEmoji() can't delegate to config.RoleEmoji() due to import cycle (config imports constants). Options: (a) move emoji constants out of constants pkg, (b) have callers use config.RoleEmoji() directly, (c) extract shared role types to a new leaf package. The env.go switch, lifecycle.go switches, cmd/role.go switches are more complex (role-specific logic, not simple property lookups) and need case-by-case analysis in future sessions."}
old_value: {"id":"gt-2e5q","title":"Role taxonomy hardcoded in 15+ switch blocks across 10+ files","description":"attached_formula: mol-polecat-work\ndispatched_by: mayor","design":"REFACTORING PLAN: (1) Consolidate 3 parallel Role constant defs into single authoritative source in internal/config/roles - extend AllRoles/TownRoles/RigRoles with role properties (autonomous, level, session-pattern, env-vars). (2) Create role registry that loads from TOML configs already in internal/config/roles/*.toml. (3) Replace switch blocks with registry lookups - e.g. registry.IsAutonomous(role), registry.SessionName(role), registry.EnvVars(role). (4) Deduplicate claude/settings.go and gemini/settings.go RoleTypeFor(). (5) Start with lowest-risk files (settings.go duplicates, runtime.go) then work outward. TOP-PRIORITY FILES: internal/claude/settings.go, internal/gemini/settings.go (identical duplicates), internal/runtime/runtime.go, internal/config/env.go (largest single switch), internal/daemon/lifecycle.go (6 switches). dispatched_by: mayor

Dogs are hardcoded to always use Haiku via an early return in resolveRoleAgentConfigCore, and excluded from TierManagedRoles. The model should be configurable per-dog or at least per cost tier. Some Dog tasks (like the Compactor doing DOLT_REBASE) may benefit from a smarter model. Add 'dog' to cost tier system or allow per-plugin model override in the plugin config.","notes":"Analysis complete. Changes needed:
1. cost_tier.go: Add 'dog' to TierManagedRoles, add dog entries to tier maps (haiku for all tiers by default, configurable via tier)
2. loader.go: Modify dog early return in resolveRoleAgentConfigCore to check cost tiers and RoleAgents[dog] for Claude overrides (not just non-Claude)  
3. plugin/types.go: Add Agent field to Plugin and PluginFrontmatter for per-plugin model override
4. daemon/handler.go: Pass plugin Agent override when dispatching to dogs
5. FormatTierRoleTable: Add dog to display list
6. Tests: Update cost_tier_test.go and add tests for dog tier integration and plugin agent override","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T04:03:40Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T06:07:35Z"}

{"id":"gt-idrl","title":"ZFC: witness DetectStalledPolecats screen-scrapes tmux pane content","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-c91dr
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T03:43:32Z
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

DetectStalledPolecats (handlers.go:1485-1514) captures 30 lines of tmux pane content and does strings.Contains matching on UI text: 'trust this folder', 'Quick safety check', 'Bypass Permissions mode'. These are third-party Claude Code TUI strings that could change with any update.

This is the second most severe ZFC violation - literal screen-scraping of another tool's display output.

Fix: Handle at startup level (ensure --dangerously-skip-permissions is always passed). If stall detection needed, use structured signals (session age vs first tool call timestamp), not raw screen scraping.","notes":"Implemented: Replaced screen-scraping (CapturePane + strings.Contains) in DetectStalledPolecats with structured signals (GetSessionCreatedUnix + GetSessionActivity). Sessions >90s old with >60s of tmux inactivity are flagged as startup-stall and remediated via AcceptStartupDialogs. No more matching against third-party TUI strings in the detection logic. Updated tests accordingly.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T02:49:19Z","created_by":"gastown/polecats/nux","updated_at":"2026-02-28T05:44:04Z"}

3. plugin/types.go: Add Agent field to Plugin and PluginFrontmatter for per-plugin model override
4. daemon/handler.go: Pass plugin Agent override when dispatching to dogs
5. FormatTierRoleTable: Add dog to display list
6. {"id":"gt-idrl","title":"ZFC: witness DetectStalledPolecats screen-scrapes tmux pane content","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-c91dr
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T03:43:32Z
dispatched_by: gastown/crew/max

DetectStalledPolecats (handlers.go:1485-1514) captures 30 lines of tmux pane content and does strings.Contains matching on UI text: 'trust this folder', 'Quick safety check', 'Bypass Permissions mode'. These are third-party Claude Code TUI strings that could change with any update.

This is the second most severe ZFC violation - literal screen-scraping of another tool's display output.

Fix: Handle at startup level (ensure --dangerously-skip-permissions is always passed). If stall detection needed, use structured signals (session age vs first tool call timestamp), not raw screen scraping.","notes":"Implemented: Replaced screen-scraping (CapturePane + strings.Contains) in DetectStalledPolecats with structured signals (GetSessionCreatedUnix + GetSessionActivity). Sessions >90s old with >60s of tmux inactivity are flagged as startup-stall and remediated via AcceptStartupDialogs. No more matching against third-party TUI strings in the detection logic. Updated tests accordingly.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T02:49:19Z","created_by":"gastown/polecats/nux","updated_at":"2026-02-28T06:32:03Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Implemented: Added struct fields and types for all previously-ignored TOML sections (extends, compose, advice, pointcuts, presets, squash, gate). Added 16 new tests covering all new sections including embedded formula integration tests. Removed integration test skip workarounds."}

Tim Sehn confirmed (2026-02-28) that DOLT_RESET --soft + DOLT_COMMIT is safe on a running server. No downtime needed. The Compactor Dog (compactor_dog.go) should be updated to:

1. Run flatten via SQL instead of stopping the server
2. Use: SET @init = (SELECT commit_hash FROM dolt_log ORDER BY date ASC LIMIT 1); CALL DOLT_RESET('--soft', @init); CALL DOLT_COMMIT('-Am', 'daily compaction');
3. Remove all server stop/start logic from compaction path
4. Auto-GC is already on (since Dolt 1.75.0) so no manual gc needed

Concurrent writes during flatten are safe - merge base shifts but diff is just the txn.

Reference: dolt-storage.md (updated 2026-02-28)"}

This is the second most severe ZFC violation - literal screen-scraping of another tool's display output.

Fix: Handle at startup level (ensure --dangerously-skip-permissions is always passed). If stall detection needed, use structured signals (session age vs first tool call timestamp), not raw screen scraping.","notes":"Implemented: Replaced screen-scraping (CapturePane + strings.Contains) in DetectStalledPolecats with structured signals (GetSessionCreatedUnix + GetSessionActivity). {"id":"gt-jmur","title":"Implement surgical interactive rebase in Compactor Dog","description":"Implement interactive rebase for surgical compaction: squash old commits while keeping recent ones individual. Based on Jason Fulghum's DOLT_REBASE procedure.

Procedure (from dolt-storage.md):
1. DOLT_BRANCH('compact-base', <init-commit>) — upstream anchor
2. DOLT_BRANCH('compact-work', 'main') + checkout
3. DOLT_REBASE('--interactive', 'compact-base') — populates dolt_rebase table
4. UPDATE dolt_rebase SET action='squash' for old commits (keep last N as 'pick')
5. DOLT_REBASE('--continue') — execute plan
6. Swap branches: delete old main, rename compact-work to main

Key constraints:
- First commit in plan must stay 'pick' (squash needs parent)
- Conflicts cause automatic abort — no manual resolution yet
- Safe on running server (Tim Sehn confirmed 2026-02-28)
- Always rebase on side branch, never directly on main

This is an ALTERNATIVE to the simple flatten. Flatten squashes everything into 1 commit. Surgical rebase keeps recent commits individual while squashing old ones.

Reference: https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2024-01-03-announcing-dolt-rebase/
See also: dolt-storage.md 'Surgical compaction' section","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T18:36:47Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T18:36:47Z"}

{"design":"# Dolt Scheduled Events for Automatic Compaction — Spike Findings

## Environment
- Dolt version: 1.82.6 (well past 1.75 auto-gc threshold)
- Server: running on port 3307, data-dir ~/.dolt-data
- Databases: beads, gastown, hq, sky, wyvern

## Test Results

### 1. Can we CREATE EVENT for daily flatten on each database?

**YES, with caveats.**

- `CREATE EVENT` works on the running Dolt sql-server
- Events support `EVERY N DAY/HOUR/MINUTE/SECOND` and `AT <timestamp>` schedules
- Events can call `CALL dolt_gc()` — tested successfully
- Events can call stored procedures (`CREATE PROCEDURE` works)
- Multi-statement `BEGIN...END` blocks work inside events
- Minimum interval: 30 seconds (Dolt enforces this floor)

**Caveat: Flatten is too complex for a single event.** The Compactor Dog's 10-step
flatten algorithm (temp branch, soft-reset, integrity check, concurrency check,
hard-reset main) requires variables, conditionals, and error handling. A stored
procedure COULD implement this, but:
- Dolt stored procedures support DECLARE, control flow, etc.
- BUT the procedure lacks the safety features of Go code (escalation, logging,
  daemon lifecycle integration)

### 2. Does it survive server restart?

**YES.** Events persist in the `dolt_schemas` table, which is part of Dolt's
versioned state. After dolt_gc() caused a server restart during testing, the
event was still present in SHOW EVENTS output.

Events are branch-specific — only events on the `main` branch execute
automatically. They must be committed to main to survive permanently.

### 3. Can it replace the Compactor Dog entirely?

**NO — not recommended.** Here's why:

| Feature | Compactor Dog | Scheduled Events |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| Threshold checking | Yes (configurable) | No (fires on schedule regardless) |
| Integrity verification | Yes (row count pre/post) | Would need stored procedure |
| Concurrency abort | Yes (HEAD movement check) | Would need stored procedure |
| Error escalation | Yes (Mayor notification) | No (silent failure) |
| Cross-database | Yes (iterates all DBs) | Per-database only |
| Logging | Yes (daemon log) | No logging infrastructure |
| Configuration | daemon.json | SQL DDL |
| GC integration | Yes (flatten then gc) | Separate event needed |
| Session safety | Own connection lifecycle | Event scheduler session |

**The fundamental mismatch:** The Compactor Dog does flatten + gc as a pipeline
with safety checks. Scheduled events are fire-and-forget SQL — no error handling,
no escalation, no observability.

### 4. What scheduled events CAN replace

**`dolt_gc()` only.** Since Dolt 1.75.0, auto-gc is ON by default. But if we
wanted explicit scheduled gc (e.g., weekly deep gc at 3 AM), a simple event works:

```sql
CREATE EVENT auto_gc_weekly
ON SCHEDULE EVERY 1 WEEK
STARTS '2026-03-01 03:00:00'
DO CALL dolt_gc();
```

**Important: `dolt_gc()` disconnects all clients.** The daemon already handles
auto-restart, so this is survivable but causes brief service interruption.

## Recommendation

1. **Keep the Compactor Dog** for flatten (complex, needs safety checks)
2. **Consider adding a scheduled event for gc** as a supplement (but auto-gc
   is already on by default in Dolt 1.82.6, so this may be redundant)
3. **Do NOT replace Compactor Dog with scheduled events** — the safety features
   (integrity checks, concurrency abort, escalation) are essential for production

## Key Technical Observations

- Events stored in `dolt_schemas` table (versioned, persistent)
- Events only fire on `main` branch
- `dolt_checkout` in an event creates a per-session branch (doesn't affect other clients
- Stored procedures work in Dolt (DECLARE, BEGIN...END, control flow)
- auto-gc is already ON by default since Dolt 1.75.0 — may make scheduled gc events redundant

{"id":"gt-v18k","title":"Explore Dolt scheduled events for automatic compaction","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-ffv926
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T18:33:04Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Tim Sehn suggested using Dolt built-in MySQL scheduled events (server-maintained cron) for automatic compaction instead of our Dog system.

Reference: https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2023-10-02-scheduled-events/

Investigate:
1. Can we CREATE EVENT for daily flatten on each database?
2. Does it survive server restart?
3. Can it replace the Compactor Dog entirely?
4. Test with a simple scheduled flatten event.

This is exploration/spike work.","design":"# Dolt Scheduled Events for Automatic Compaction — Spike Findings

## Environment
- Dolt version: 1.82.6 (well past 1.75 auto-gc threshold)
- Server: running on port 3307, data-dir ~/.dolt-data
- Databases: beads, gastown, hq, sky, wyvern

## Test Results

### 1. Can we CREATE EVENT for daily flatten on each database?

**YES, with caveats.**

- `CREATE EVENT` works on the running Dolt sql-server
- Events support `EVERY N DAY/HOUR/MINUTE/SECOND` and `AT <timestamp>` schedules
- Events can call `CALL dolt_gc()` — tested successfully
- Events can call stored procedures (`CREATE PROCEDURE` works)
- Multi-statement `BEGIN...END` blocks work inside events
- Minimum interval: 30 seconds (Dolt enforces this floor)

**Caveat: Flatten is too complex for a single event.** The Compactor Dog's 10-step
flatten algorithm (temp branch, soft-reset, integrity check, concurrency check,
hard-reset main) requires variables, conditionals, and error handling. A stored
procedure COULD implement this, but:
- Dolt stored procedures support DECLARE, control flow, etc.
- BUT the procedure lacks the safety features of Go code (escalation, logging,
  daemon lifecycle integration)

### 2. Does it survive server restart?

**YES.** Events persist in the `dolt_schemas` table, which is part of Dolt's
versioned state. After dolt_gc() caused a server restart during testing, the
event was still present in SHOW EVENTS output.

Events are branch-specific — only events on the `main` branch execute
automatically. They must be committed to main to survive permanently.

### 3. Can it replace the Compactor Dog entirely?

**NO — not recommended.** Here's why:

| Feature | Compactor Dog | Scheduled Events |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| Threshold checking | Yes (configurable) | No (fires on schedule regardless) |
| Integrity verification | Yes (row count pre/post) | Would need stored procedure |
| Concurrency abort | Yes (HEAD movement check) | Would need stored procedure |
| Error escalation | Yes (Mayor notification) | No (silent failure) |
| Cross-database | Yes (iterates all DBs) | Per-database only |
| Logging | Yes (daemon log) | No logging infrastructure |
| Configuration | daemon.json | SQL DDL |
| GC integration | Yes (flatten then gc) | Separate event needed |
| Session safety | Own connection lifecycle | Event scheduler session |

**The fundamental mismatch:** The Compactor Dog does flatten + gc as a pipeline
with safety checks. Scheduled events are fire-and-forget SQL — no error handling,
no escalation, no observability.

### 4. What scheduled events CAN replace

**`dolt_gc()` only.** Since Dolt 1.75.0, auto-gc is ON by default. But if we
wanted explicit scheduled gc (e.g., weekly deep gc at 3 AM), a simple event works:

```sql
CREATE EVENT auto_gc_weekly
ON SCHEDULE EVERY 1 WEEK
STARTS '2026-03-01 03:00:00'
DO CALL dolt_gc();
```

**Important: `dolt_gc()` disconnects all clients.** The daemon already handles
auto-restart, so this is survivable but causes brief service interruption.

## Recommendation

1. **Keep the Compactor Dog** for flatten (complex, needs safety checks)
2. **Consider adding a scheduled event for gc** as a supplement (but auto-gc
   is already on by default in Dolt 1.82.6, so this may be redundant)
3. **Do NOT replace Compactor Dog with scheduled events** — the safety features
   (integrity checks, concurrency abort, escalation) are essential for production

## Key Technical Observations

- Events stored in `dolt_schemas` table (versioned, persistent)
- Events only fire on `main` branch
- `dolt_checkout` in an event creates a per-session branch (doesn't affect other clients
- Stored procedures work in Dolt (DECLARE, BEGIN...END, control flow)
- auto-gc is already ON by default since Dolt 1.75.0 — may make scheduled gc events redundant
DESIGN

Two components: (1) CLI command 'gt dolt rebase \u003cdb\u003e' with --keep-recent, --dry-run, --yes-i-am-sure flags; (2) Daemon integration via CompactorDogConfig mode='surgical' and keep_recent fields. Both have integrity verification, concurrency checks, and cleanup on failure. Follows the Dolt DOLT_REBASE procedure from dolt-storage.md."}

{"id":"gt-jmur","title":"Implement surgical interactive rebase in Compactor Dog","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-v4uyqk
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T18:36:55Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Implement interactive rebase for surgical compaction: squash old commits while keeping recent ones individual. Based on Jason Fulghum's DOLT_REBASE procedure.

Procedure (from dolt-storage.md):
1. DOLT_BRANCH('compact-base', <init-commit>) — upstream anchor
2. DOLT_BRANCH('compact-work', 'main') + checkout
3. DOLT_REBASE('--interactive', 'compact-base') — populates dolt_rebase table
4. UPDATE dolt_rebase SET action='squash' for old commits (keep last N as 'pick')
5. DOLT_REBASE('--continue') — execute plan
6. Swap branches: delete old main, rename compact-work to main

Key constraints:
- First commit in plan must stay 'pick' (squash needs parent)
- Conflicts cause automatic abort — no manual resolution yet
- Safe on running server (Tim Sehn confirmed 2026-02-28)
- Always rebase on side branch, never directly on main

This is an ALTERNATIVE to the simple flatten. Flatten squashes everything into 1 commit. Surgical rebase keeps recent commits individual while squashing old ones.

Reference: https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2024-01-03-announcing-dolt-rebase/
See also: dolt-storage.md 'Surgical compaction' section","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"gastown/polecats/rictus","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T18:36:47Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T18:38:45Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Implemented: Simplified all three flatten implementations (compactor_dog.go, maintain.go, dolt_flatten.go) to use direct SQL on running server. Removed temp-branch approach, CLI-based dolt gc, server stop/start, and rig parking. -292 lines net reduction."}

{"id":"gt-bvse","title":"Compactor Dog: use SQL-based flatten on running server","description":"attached_molecule: gt-wisp-u5wkgj
attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
attached_at: 2026-02-28T18:32:51Z
dispatched_by: mayor

Tim Sehn confirmed (2026-02-28) that DOLT_RESET --soft + DOLT_COMMIT is safe on a running server. No downtime needed. The Compactor Dog (compactor_dog.go) should be updated to:

1. Run flatten via SQL instead of stopping the server
2. Use: SET @init = (SELECT commit_hash FROM dolt_log ORDER BY date ASC LIMIT 1); CALL DOLT_RESET('--soft', @init); CALL DOLT_COMMIT('-Am', 'daily compaction');
3. Remove all server stop/start logic from compaction path
4. Auto-GC is already on (since Dolt 1.75.0) so no manual gc needed

Concurrent writes during flatten are safe - merge base shifts but diff is just the txn.

Reference: dolt-storage.md (updated 2026-02-28)","notes":"Analysis: Three flatten implementations all use temp-branch approach (compactor_dog.go, maintain.go, dolt_flatten.go). - `CREATE EVENT` works on the running Dolt sql-server
- Events support `EVERY N DAY/HOUR/MINUTE/SECOND` and `AT <timestamp>` schedules
- Events can call `CALL dolt_gc()` — tested successfully
- Events can call stored procedures (`CREATE PROCEDURE` works)
- Multi-statement `BEGIN...END` blocks work inside events
- Minimum interval: 30 seconds (Dolt enforces this floor)

**Caveat: Flatten is too complex for a single event.** The Compactor Dog's 10-step
flatten algorithm (temp branch, soft-reset, integrity check, concurrency check,
hard-reset main) requires variables, conditionals, and error handling. A stored
procedure COULD implement this, but:
- Dolt stored procedures support DECLARE, control flow, etc.
- BUT the procedure lacks the safety features of Go code (escalation, logging,
  daemon lifecycle integration)

### 2. Does it survive server restart?

**YES.** Events persist in the `dolt_schemas` table, which is part of Dolt's
versioned state. After dolt_gc() caused a server restart during testing, the
event was still present in SHOW EVENTS output.

Events are branch-specific — only events on the `main` branch execute
automatically. They must be committed to main to survive permanently.

### 3. Can it replace the Compactor Dog entirely?

**NO — not recommended.** Here's why:

| Feature | Compactor Dog | Scheduled Events |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| Threshold checking | Yes (configurable) | No (fires on schedule regardless) |
| Integrity verification | Yes (row count pre/post) | Would need stored procedure |
| Concurrency abort | Yes (HEAD movement check) | Would need stored procedure |
| Error escalation | Yes (Mayor notification) | No (silent failure) |
| Cross-database | Yes (iterates all DBs) | Per-database only |
| Logging | Yes (daemon log) | No logging infrastructure |
| Configuration | daemon.json | SQL DDL |
| GC integration | Yes (flatten then gc) | Separate event needed |
| Session safety | Own connection lifecycle | Event scheduler session |

**The fundamental mismatch:** The Compactor Dog does flatten + gc as a pipeline
with safety checks. Scheduled events are fire-and-forget SQL — no error handling,
no escalation, no observability.

### 4. What scheduled events CAN replace

**`dolt_gc()` only.** Since Dolt 1.75.0, auto-gc is ON by default. But if we
wanted explicit scheduled gc (e.g., weekly deep gc at 3 AM), a simple event works:

```sql
CREATE EVENT auto_gc_weekly
ON SCHEDULE EVERY 1 WEEK
STARTS '2026-03-01 03:00:00'
DO CALL dolt_gc();
```

**Important: `dolt_gc()` disconnects all clients.** The daemon already handles
auto-restart, so this is survivable but causes brief service interruption.

## Recommendation

1. **Keep the Compactor Dog** for flatten (complex, needs safety checks)
2. **Consider adding a scheduled event for gc** as a supplement (but auto-gc
   is already on by default in Dolt 1.82.6, so this may be redundant)
3. **Do NOT replace Compactor Dog with scheduled events** — the safety features
   (integrity checks, concurrency abort, escalation) are essential for production

## Key Technical Observations

- Events stored in `dolt_schemas` table (versioned, persistent)
- Events only fire on `main` branch
- `dolt_checkout` in an event creates a per-session branch (doesn't affect other clients
- Stored procedures work in Dolt (DECLARE, BEGIN...END, control flow)
- auto-gc is already ON by default since Dolt 1.75.0 — may make scheduled gc events redundant
DESIGN
)","notes":"Implemented: Spike complete. Tested Dolt scheduled events on 1.82.6. Events work and persist but cannot replace Compactor Dog. Findings documented in docs/design/dolt-storage.md.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"gastown/polecats/nux","owner":"steve.yegge@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-02-28T18:32:33Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-28T18:44:15Z"}

{"description":"Implement interactive rebase for surgical compaction: squash old commits while keeping recent ones individual. Based on Jason Fulghum's DOLT_REBASE procedure.

Procedure (from dolt-storage.md):
1. DOLT_BRANCH('compact-base', <init-commit>) — upstream anchor
2. DOLT_BRANCH('compact-work', 'main') + checkout
3. DOLT_REBASE('--interactive', 'compact-base') — populates dolt_rebase table
4. UPDATE dolt_rebase SET action='squash' for old commits (keep last N as 'pick')
5. DOLT_REBASE('--continue') — execute plan
6. Swap branches: delete old main, rename compact-work to main

Key constraints:
- First commit in plan must stay 'pick' (squash needs parent)
- Conflicts cause automatic abort — no manual resolution yet
- Safe on running server (Tim Sehn confirmed 2026-02-28)
- Always rebase on side branch, never directly on main

This is an ALTERNATIVE to the simple flatten. Flatten squashes everything into 1 commit. Surgical rebase keeps recent commits individual while squashing old ones.

Reference: https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2024-01-03-announcing-dolt-rebase/
See also: dolt-storage.md 'Surgical compaction' section"}

attached_formula: mol-polecat-work
